en.extract:
The little friarbird (Philemon citreogularis), also known as the little leatherhead or yellow-throated friarbird, is the smallest of the friarbirds within the Philemon genus. It is found throughout northern and eastern Australia as well as southern Papua New Guinea. It lives a very prominent life, whereby it can easily be seen chasing other honeyeaters, and also it is very vocal. However, the little friarbird is usually spotted high up in trees, rarely being seen on the ground.


Description
The little friarbird ranges from 25–30 centimetres (9.8–11.8 in) in length. The average size tends to be 27 centimetres (11 in) with an average weight of 67 grams (2.4 oz). Although males and females tend to be very similar in appearance, males are larger.
The little friarbird can be easily distinguished from other friarbirds by the absence of a casque (a prominent ridge or bump present on the beak). Another key recognisable feature is the bare blue skin present under the eye which widens over the cheek. (The bare blue skin can vary from grey to black in tone.) Dark feathers are present both above and below the blue skin. The top of the head and body are a dark grey-brown with a dull white fringe present on the nape which flows around to a wide patch on the side of the neck. Fine silky white feathers are present under the chin with silvery white streaks flowing down the breast merging to pale grey for the underbody of the little friarbird.
The little friarbird also has a black curved bill, dark brown eyes with bluish black legs. Its flight characteristics involve wing-beats which are shallow and quivering in nature. The tail is clearly square-cut when spread with slight pale tips present on the tail feathers.


Juveniles
Juvenile little friarbirds tend to have a paler appearance with a lighter facial skin. The chin, throat and, in some cases, upper breast tend to appear as a washed yellow with yellow spots on the side of the breast. The term 'citreogularis' is frequently used to describe young birds which have a yellow fore-neck. The tail feathers also tend to appear with a shallower cut, unlike those of adult little friarbirds.


Voice
The little friarbird has a very distinct voice consisting of a repeated liquid mellow "gee-wit" or "chewip". When breeding, the song is extended and includes chattering scoldings. A common sequence may be paraphrased as "rackety-crookshank".


Diet
The little friarbird has been observed feeding alone, in pairs and small flocks. However, they are rarely seen feeding on the ground as they prefer to remain in the trees and feed with other honeyeaters in mixed groups. They feed on nectar, blossoms, fruit, invertebrates, and sometimes flowers and seeds.


Distribution and habitat
The little friarbird is present most predominantly in northern Australia with seasonal movements to south-east Australia. They have been witnessed from the district of Broome in Western Australia through the Kimberleys into the northern portion of the Northern Territory. They have been sighted throughout most of Queensland going as far inland as Mount Isa and Opalton. In summer, they also spread throughout the south-east of Australia inhabiting the states of New South Wales, Victoria and South Australia, and the Australian Capital Territory.
The little friarbird has been sighted along the Murray River and throughout the Riverina within New South Wales. In Victoria, the little friarbird is seen in the north-central region of the state, and near prominent bodies of water, such as Hattah Lakes. Sightings occur as far as Morgan on the Murray River in South Australia. They are also present in southern Papua New Guinea and on several islands adjacent to Australia.
The little friarbird is found most predominantly around water bodies. They can be seen in swamp woodlands, mangroves, shrub communities, open forests and woodlands dominated by Eucalyptus tree species. They also inhabit orchards, vineyards and gardens during a good flowering season. They may also inhabit arid zones, but only where water bodies extend well into the area.


Breeding
Little friarbirds form monogamous pairs to breed. Breeding records vary with the earliest being witnessed in July through to the latest in February. Pairs share the role of building the nest which is a deep open cup that is flimsy and able to be seen through (eggs are visible). The nest comprises a large variety of materials including bark fiber, fine grass, spiders' web, rootlets, and hair.  The nest is usually built over a water body low in tree branches, 2–10 meters high, and suspended from twigs or within dropped foliage. A clutch usually consist of 2–3 eggs (sometimes 4), which tend to be an oval to tapered oval shape, averaging 20 by 27 millimetres (0.79 in × 1.06 in) in size. The eggs appear chestnut in colour and can be a whitish pink to salmon red, or spotted purplish-red to purple. Up to 2 broods can be raised within a single breeding season with the female being the sole incubator of the eggs. A single nesting period tends to take 14 days within which 13 days of incubation occurs. During the breeding season, the little friarbird is often parasitized by the common koel (Eudynamys scolopacea), a common cuckoo species.


Gallery


References


External links

Recordings of little friarbird from Graeme Chapman's sound library
Recordings of little friarbird from Xeno-canto sound archive

The '''little friarbird''' ('''''Philemon citreogularis'''''), also known as the '''little leatherhead''' or '''yellow-throated friarbird''', is the smallest of the friarbirds within the Philemon genus.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=A Field Guide to the Birds of Australia|last=Pizzey|first=Graham|publisher=Harper Collins|year=1991|isbn=0-00-219205-5|location=Sydney|pages=320–321}}</ref> It is found throughout northern and eastern Australia as well as southern Papua New Guinea.<ref name=":0" /> It lives a very prominent life, whereby it can easily be seen chasing other honeyeaters, and also it is very vocal.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=http://www.birdlife.org.au/bird-profile/little-friarbird|title=Little Friarbird|website=Birdlife Australia|access-date=9 June 2017}}</ref> However, the little friarbird is usually spotted high up in trees, rarely being seen on the ground.<ref name=":1" />

== Description ==
The little friarbird ranges from {{convert|25|–|30|cm|in}} in length.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|url=http://www.australianbushbirds.info/infp/philemon_citreogularis.html|title=Little Friarbird - Australian Bush Birds|last=Jones|first=Raymond M|website=www.australianbushbirds.info|language=en|access-date=2017-06-09}}</ref> The average size tends to be {{convert|27|cm|in}} with an average weight of {{convert|67|g|oz}}.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/species/Philemon-citreogularis|title=Little Friarbird {{!}} BIRDS in BACKYARDS|website=www.birdsinbackyards.net|access-date=2017-06-09}}</ref> Although males and females tend to be very similar in appearance, males are larger.<ref name=":1" />

The little friarbird can be easily distinguished from other friarbirds by the absence of a casque (a prominent ridge or bump present on the beak).<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|title=Birds of Australia|last=Macdonald|first=J|publisher=A. H. & A. W. Reed Pty Ltd.|year=1973|isbn=0-589-07117-3|location=Sydney|page=426}}</ref> Another key recognisable feature is the bare blue skin present under the eye which widens over the cheek. (The bare blue skin can vary from grey to black in tone.)<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite book|title=The Field Guide to the Birds of Australia|last=Pizzey|first=Graham|publisher=Harper Collins|year=2007|isbn=978-0-207-19935-6|location=Sydney|pages=380}}</ref><ref name=":6">{{Cite book|title=Field Guide to the Birds of Australia|author=Simpson, K.|author2=Day, N.|name-list-style=amp|publisher=Penguin books Australia|year=1996|isbn=0-670-86305-X|location=Melbourne|pages=190–191}}</ref> Dark feathers are present both above and below the blue skin.<ref name=":6" /> The top of the head and body are a dark grey-brown with a dull white fringe present on the nape which flows around to a wide patch on the side of the neck.<ref name=":4" /> Fine silky white feathers are present under the chin with silvery white streaks flowing down the breast merging to pale grey for the underbody of the little friarbird.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":4" />

The little friarbird also has a black curved bill, dark brown eyes with bluish black legs.<ref name=":4" /> Its flight characteristics involve wing-beats which are shallow and quivering in nature.<ref name=":0" /> The tail is clearly square-cut when spread with slight pale tips present on the tail feathers.<ref name=":0" />

===Juveniles===
Juvenile little friarbirds tend to have a paler appearance with a lighter facial skin.<ref name=":6" /> The chin, throat and, in some cases, upper breast tend to appear as a washed yellow with yellow spots on the side of the breast.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":6" /> The term 'citreogularis' is frequently used to describe young birds which have a yellow fore-neck.<ref name=":4" /> The tail feathers also tend to appear with a shallower cut, unlike those of adult little friarbirds.<ref name=":4" />

===Voice===
The little friarbird has a very distinct voice consisting of a repeated liquid mellow "gee-wit" or "chewip".<ref name=":0" /> When breeding, the song is extended and includes chattering scoldings.<ref name=":0" /> A common sequence may be paraphrased as "rackety-crookshank".<ref name=Morcombe />

== Diet ==
The little friarbird has been observed feeding alone, in pairs and small flocks.<ref name=":3" /> However, they are rarely seen feeding on the ground as they prefer to remain in the trees and feed with other honeyeaters in [[mixed species flocks|mixed groups]].<ref name=":3" /> They feed on nectar, blossoms, fruit, invertebrates, and sometimes flowers and seeds.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5" />

==Distribution and habitat==
The little friarbird is present most predominantly in northern Australia with seasonal movements to south-east Australia.<ref name=":3" /> They have been witnessed from the district of [[Broome, Western Australia|Broome]] in Western Australia through the [[Kimberley (Western Australia)|Kimberleys]] into the northern portion of the [[Northern Territory]].<ref name=":0" /> They have been sighted throughout most of [[Queensland]] going as far inland as [[Mount Isa]] and Opalton.<ref name=":0" /> In summer, they also spread throughout the south-east of Australia inhabiting the states of [[New South Wales]], [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]] and [[South Australia]], and the [[Australian Capital Territory]].<ref name=":0" />

The little friarbird has been sighted along the [[Murray River]] and throughout the [[Riverina]] within New South Wales.<ref name=":0" /> In Victoria, the little friarbird is seen in the north-central region of the state, and near prominent bodies of water, such as [[Hattah-Kulkyne National Park|Hattah Lakes]].<ref name=":0" /> Sightings occur as far as Morgan on the Murray River in South Australia.<ref name=":0" /> They are also present in southern [[Papua New Guinea]] and on several islands adjacent to Australia.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":5" />

The little friarbird is found most predominantly around water bodies.<ref name=":0" /> They can be seen in swamp woodlands, mangroves, shrub communities, open forests and woodlands dominated by [[Eucalyptus]] tree species.<ref name=":3" /> They also inhabit orchards, vineyards and gardens during a good flowering season.<ref name=":0" /> They may also inhabit arid zones, but only where water bodies extend well into the area.<ref name=":3" />

== Breeding ==
Little friarbirds form monogamous pairs to breed.<ref name=":1" /> Breeding records vary with the earliest being witnessed in July through to the latest in February.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5" /> Pairs share the role of building the nest which is a deep open cup that is flimsy and able to be seen through (eggs are visible).<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4" /> The nest comprises a large variety of materials including bark fiber, fine grass, spiders' web, rootlets, and hair.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" />  The nest is usually built over a water body low in tree branches, 2–10 meters high, and suspended from twigs or within dropped foliage.<ref name=":0" /> A clutch usually consist of 2–3 eggs (sometimes 4), which tend to be an oval to tapered oval shape, averaging {{convert|20|x|27|mm|in}} in size.<ref name=Morcombe>Morcombe, Michael (2012) ''Field Guide to Australian Birds''. Pascal Press, Glebe, NSW. Revised edition. {{ISBN|978174021417-9}}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":5" /> The eggs appear chestnut in colour and can be a whitish pink to salmon red, or spotted purplish-red to purple.<ref name=":0" /> Up to 2 broods can be raised within a single breeding season with the female being the sole incubator of the eggs.<ref name=":2" /> A single nesting period tends to take 14 days within which 13 days of incubation occurs.<ref name=":3" /> During the breeding season, the little friarbird is often [[brood parasite|parasitized]] by the common koel (''Eudynamys scolopacea''), a common cuckoo species.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" />

zh.extract:
小吮蜜鳥（學名：Philemon citreogularis）是一種分佈於新畿內亞南部和澳大利亞東部林地的鳥類，屬雀形目吸蜜鳥科。它是該屬鳥種最小的，喙上也沒有突起。


參考資料
BirdLife International. Philemon citreogularis. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2004.   [12 November 2006] （英语）.  Database entry includes justification for why this species is of least concern
